name: Multi-Tenant AI SaaS
description: Attack tenant isolation in shared AI infrastructure. Explore cross-tenant context leakage, tenant privilege escalation, shared model cache poisoning, and how multi-tenant vector databases fail to enforce data boundaries.
author: DVAP Research Team
objectives: Leak context from Tenant A into Tenant B's session
Escalate from standard tenant to admin tenant privileges
Poison a shared embedding cache to affect other tenants
Extract another tenant's stored documents from a shared vector store
Demonstrate tenant identifier manipulation to access foreign data
attack_surface: Tenant identifier passed in user-controlled request headers
Shared vector store without strict namespace isolation
Shared model prompt cache with weak key isolation
Admin API accessible with predictable tenant tokens
Cross-tenant RAG queries without filter enforcement
mitigations: Strict namespace isolation in vector stores
Tenant-scoped cache keys with integrity protection
Mandatory tenant filters on all data queries
detection_opportunities: Monitor for tenant ID manipulation attempts
Alert on cross-tenant data access patterns
Audit all vector store queries with tenant attribution
Alert on privilege escalation to admin tenant

architecture.data_flow: Request + Tenant ID -> Router -> Tenant context assembly -> Shared LLM -> Tenant-scoped response

threat_actors: Tenant admin attempting to access other tenants
threat_model.impact: Cross-tenant data breach, tenant impersonation, shared infrastructure compromise
threat_model.likelihood: Critical in multi-tenant SaaS without strict isolation

challenges.name: Neighbor's Data
challenges.description: Extract a document belonging to Tenant ID 'acme-corp' while authenticated as 'attacker-org'.
hints: The tenant ID is included in the API request body
Vector store queries filter by tenant but the filter is advisory

challenges.name: Shared Cache Attack
challenges.description: Poison a shared prompt cache entry to serve your malicious response to requests from a different tenant.
hints: Cache keys include a hash of the system prompt + user message
Tenants with identical system prompts share cache entries
